question: NACE 2008 Student Manual! START OF CHAPTER 1 Corrosion is an electrochemical process involving
Metal loss or corrosion occurs at the

options.B: cathode to anode via electronic path

question: Electrons flow in a corrosion cell by which of the following paths?
At the cathodic surface in a corrosion cell, the electrons from the ____ meet ____ ions from the solution.
Oxidation of the metal occurs at the ____ surfaces and reduction of ____ ions occurs at the ____.
Is oxidation necessarily associated with oxygen?

options.A: anode to metallic path to cathode
options.B: anode to electronic path to cathode

question: Current flow in a corrosion cell flows which path: Voltage is the measure of the electrochemical difference between two electrodes in an electrolyte

options.A: at the anode and the cathode

question: As current is impressed on a metal over time, where does polarization occur?

Passivity can be defined as the
The ____ region is when a slight increase in corrosion potential causes the corrosion current to increase
The ____ region is when the corrosion potential increases to a certain point, however, the corrosion current suddenly decreases and remain essentially constant

For the active (or nonpassivating) metal, as corrosion potential _____, corrosion current ____, more or less logarithmically (or linearly on a semi- log graph).
This type of film which forms naturally on most metals when they are exposed to the air, can provide substantial protection against further attack by many environments

Type of corrosion with an even loss of metal over the entire area or a large area of the structure F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack g This type of corrosion occurs where two tightly spaced surfaces - either two metals or a metal and nonmetal - are exposed to a corroding environment. Holes, gasket surfaces, lap joints, surface deposits, and crevices are likely places for this type of corrosion. F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack
This corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals are connected. The potential of the two metals creates a voltage difference, the driving force for corrosion. F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack
Localized corrosion that occurs over a small area of the metal surface and leaves holes in the surface. F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack

This is a local corrosion attack at the grain boundaries of an alloy. It is caused by a difference in element composition near the boundaries, compared to the rest of the alloy. F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack
This type of corrosion involves the certain removal of one of the elements from an alloy. F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack
This corrosion is associated with rapid movement of a corrosive fluid over a metal, removing corrosion products. F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack
results from the reaction of the metal with a corrosive environment and the presence of a stress F) crevice corrosion G) uniform attack

The cracking of a metal under an applied tensile stress in the presence of a corrosive environment
